metadata.summary: Integration-test Rust HTTP services with tower::ServiceExt::oneshot and testcontainers-rs Postgres; isolate per-test DB state with per-test schema or transaction rollback.

content.title: Rust Integration Testing — Axum/Tower and Testcontainers
content.summary: Integration tests for Rust HTTP services drive the real Axum (or Actix) router via tower::ServiceExt::oneshot without binding a port. Database tests use testcontainers-rs to spin up a per-test Postgres container — or a shared container with per-test schema isolation — ensuring tests never depend on a shared dev DB and never leave state that breaks parallel runs.

why.p: Unit tests with mockall verify logic but cannot catch wiring mistakes: wrong handler registration, missing middleware, broken serialization, or a migration that broke the schema. Integration tests drive the full request path and expose these errors early. tower::ServiceExt::oneshot is the canonical Axum pattern: it calls the router directly in the test process with no network socket, so tests are fast and portable.

testcontainers-rs starts a real Postgres (or any Docker image) per test or shared across the suite. Per-test containers guarantee isolation at the cost of startup time; a shared container with per-test schema (CREATE SCHEMA test_<uuid>; DROP SCHEMA ... CASCADE after) is the common middle ground for CI speed.

list.item: Rust services using Axum/Actix/Tonic where you need confidence that the real router, extractors, middleware, and DB round-trip work end-to-end.
Any handler that reads or writes a database and where a mock would just assert the mock setup.
Pagination, filtering, and ordering logic that requires real SQL execution to be meaningful.
Scenarios where multiple components interact and the unit-level boundaries are wrong.
Business logic that has no I/O — a unit test with mockall is cheaper and faster.
Hot-path benchmarks — cargo test is not cargo bench; use criterion for perf-sensitive code.
Cross-compile targets where running tests on the host does not reflect the target. Use cross + Docker, or skip and rely on CI.

rule.statement: Use tower::ServiceExt::oneshot to drive Axum routers in integration tests. Never bind a real TCP listener on a fixed port (e.g., 127.0.0.1:8080) for HTTP integration tests. If a real listener is needed, bind to :0 and discover the assigned port with local_addr().
rule.rationale: Hardcoded ports cause CI failures when tests run in parallel — two tests bind the same address and one panics. oneshot drives the router in-process with no socket allocation.

rule.statement: Use testcontainers-rs for any integration test that requires a real database. Never point integration tests at a shared dev or staging database. Provide per-test schema isolation (CREATE SCHEMA test_<uuid> + DROP after) when sharing a container across a test suite.
rule.rationale: Integration tests that share a real Postgres container without isolation fail in CI in random order because one test's INSERT is visible to another's SELECT. Per-test schema or transaction rollback prevents state leak.

rule.statement: Run database migrations (sqlx migrate run or diesel migration run) inside the integration test setup before any assertions. Tests MUST NOT assume migrations are pre-applied in the container.
rule.rationale: A freshly-started Postgres container has no schema. Tests that skip migration setup produce confusing "relation does not exist" errors that are hard to diagnose in CI.

section.name: axum-integration-pattern
section.p: The canonical shape of an Axum integration test: build the app in a setup_app() helper that creates a test database pool and runs migrations; drive requests with app.oneshot(Request::builder()...build()).await; assert status code and deserialize the body with serde_json.

example.title: Axum integration test with tower::ServiceExt::oneshot
example.note: oneshot consumes the router once; call setup_app() once per test, not once for the suite. This guarantees each test starts with a clean router state.

antipattern.description: Integration tests sharing a Postgres container without truncation or transaction-rollback between tests.
antipattern.reason: Tests fail in CI in random order because INSERT from one test is visible to another's SELECT or constraint check. Isolation must be explicit: per-test schema, per-test container, or BEGIN ... ROLLBACK wrapping.

antipattern.description: Binding a real TCP listener on a hardcoded port (e.g., 127.0.0.1:8080) in integration tests.
antipattern.reason: Parallel test runs (cargo nextest) assign multiple tests to the same port and one panics with "address already in use". Use oneshot or bind to :0.

section.name: snapshot-testing
section.p: For complex output — rendered emails, AST pretty-prints, serialized API responses — insta snapshot tests keep the test body readable and make review-friendly diffs. Run cargo insta review in the PR to update snapshots deliberately.

rule.statement: Use insta for snapshot tests of complex structured output (HTML, large JSON, AST). Do not hand-write multi-line assert_eq! strings for outputs longer than ~10 lines.
rule.rationale: Hand-written expected strings break on every whitespace or ordering change, producing noisy diffs. insta stores the expected value in a .snap file that git tracks and reviews cleanly.

checklist.check: setup_app() creates a fresh database and runs migrations before returning.
Each test calls setup_app() independently — no shared router state.
No hardcoded TCP ports — use oneshot or bind to :0.
Database isolation is explicit: per-test schema, per-test container, or rollback.
Complex output tests use insta snapshots instead of hand-written assert_eq! strings.
wiremock used for external HTTP dependencies in integration scope.
